g.data-narration: Contiguous Memory Allocation is a fundamental OS memory management technique where each process gets a single continuous block of memory.
Memory is divided into two partitions: the OS at low addresses and user processes at higher addresses. The OS must protect itself and each process.
Base and Limit registers provide hardware memory protection. The base holds the starting address and limit defines the size of the process's memory.
Fixed partitioning divides memory into predetermined partitions at system startup. Simple but inflexible - the number of partitions limits multiprogramming.
Internal fragmentation occurs in fixed partitioning when a process gets a partition larger than it needs. The wasted space inside the partition is lost forever.
Variable partitioning creates partitions dynamically to fit each process exactly. Memory is allocated precisely to the process size, reducing waste.
External fragmentation happens in variable partitioning when free memory exists but is not contiguous. Small scattered holes cannot be used for large processes.
g.text: Total free = 480KB but request of 500KB FAILS - not contiguous!

g.data-narration: First Fit allocates the first hole large enough for the process. It's the fastest method because it stops searching once it finds a suitable hole.
Best Fit finds the smallest hole that fits - leaves tiny unusable fragments. Worst Fit takes the largest hole - causes fastest fragmentation.
First fit is generally preferred - it's fastest and provides good memory utilization. Best fit minimizes wasted space but is slower.
g.text: 50-Percent Rule: ~0.5N blocks lost to fragmentation in First Fit

g.data-narration: Compaction solves external fragmentation by moving all processes together, combining all free space into one large contiguous hole.
g.text: Limitation: Requires dynamic relocation (relocation register). Expensive operation!

g.data-narration: Memory protection uses base and limit registers. Fixed partitioning causes internal fragmentation while variable partitioning leads to external fragmentation.
For exams: know first fit vs best fit tracing, internal vs external fragmentation differences, base-limit protection mechanism, and how paging solves fragmentation.
g.text: • First Fit vs Best Fit vs Worst Fit - trace through examples
• Internal (fixed) vs External (variable) fragmentation - know the difference
• Draw memory diagrams for allocation strategies and compaction
First Fit preferred: fastest + good utilization
